name: Gospel of Thomas
description: The Gospel of Thomas is a non-canonical sayings gospel discovered in 1945 near Nag Hammadi, Egypt, as part of the Nag Hammadi library. It consists of 114 sayings attributed to Jesus, many of which bear resemblance to sayings found in the canonical Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John), while others are unique. Proponents often view it as an independent source of Jesus' teachings, potentially predating or existing alongside the canonical Gospels. Some scholars suggest it offers a glimpse into an earlier, less dogmatic form of Christianity, potentially Gnostic-influenced. They highlight sayings that emphasize self-knowledge and inner spiritual understanding. Mainstream scholarship generally views the Gospel of Thomas as a secondary source, likely written in the 2nd century CE, drawing upon and adapting material already present in the synoptic Gospels and potentially other oral traditions. While acknowledging its value for understanding the diversity of early Christian thought, most scholars do not consider it an independent witness to the historical Jesus. Its unique sayings are often attributed to later theological developments and interpretations rather than direct transmission from Jesus. Debates center on whether the Gospel of Thomas represents an independent tradition, a derivative work, or a combination of both. The dating of the text and its relationship to other early Christian writings remain subjects of ongoing scholarly discussion. The Gospel's emphasis on gnosis (knowledge) and its perceived lack of narrative elements contribute to its ongoing fascination and diverse interpretations.
subtitle: A collection of sayings attributed to Jesus.

claims.claim: The Gospel of Thomas represents an independent source of Jesus' teachings.
claims.evidence: The presence of unique sayings not found in the canonical Gospels, and arguments for its early dating.
claims.criticism: Mainstream scholarship generally dates the Gospel of Thomas to the 2nd century CE, later than the canonical Gospels, and suggests it draws upon and adapts existing traditions.

claims.claim: The Gospel of Thomas offers insight into an earlier, less dogmatic form of Christianity.
claims.evidence: Its emphasis on self-knowledge and inner spiritual understanding, and perceived lack of narrative elements.
claims.criticism: The 'less dogmatic' interpretation is subjective. Scholars argue that the Gospel of Thomas reflects specific theological perspectives, potentially Gnostic-influenced, rather than a universally earlier form of Christianity.

claims.claim: Some sayings in the Gospel of Thomas are more authentic than similar sayings in the canonical Gospels.
claims.evidence: Arguments based on textual criticism and the perceived simplicity or originality of certain sayings.
claims.criticism: Determining authenticity is highly subjective. The canonical Gospels have a stronger manuscript tradition and are generally considered more reliable sources for the historical Jesus.

keyFigures.name: Elaine Pagels
keyFigures.role: Scholar who has argued for the importance of the Gospel of Thomas in understanding early Christian diversity.

keyFigures.name: Bart D. Ehrman
keyFigures.role: Scholar who argues that the Gospel of Thomas is a secondary source dependent on the canonical Gospels and oral traditions.

timeline.event: Discovery of the Nag Hammadi library, including the Gospel of Thomas.
Estimated date of composition by most mainstream scholars.

sources.title: Bart D. Ehrman
